2000-02-01.title: 3 Israeli Soldiers Killed in Lebanon, Marring Peace Effort
2000-02-01.abstract: Three Israeli soldiers are killed in southern Lebanon as Iranian-backed guerrillas continue to escalate attacks during lull in peace negotiations between Israel and Syria, Lebanon's power broker; Israeli planes respond with artillery attacks and air strikes on Hezbollah guerrilla targets in southern Lebanon; photo (M)

2000-02-01.title: Japan Puts Gas-Attack Sect Under Scrutiny
2000-02-01.abstract: Public commission in Japan rules Aum Shinrikyo religious sect, held responsible for nerve gas attack against Tokyo subway station in 1995 that killed 12 people, still poses threat to society; instructs security authorities to put sect under surveillance for up to three years (S)

2000-02-01.title: Inefficient English Programs Being Revised : Koreans Redouble Efforts To Conquer Language Bar
2000-02-01.abstract: SEOUL—  In a country where everyone studies English grammar from primary school through high school, Koreans are awakening to a disturbing reality: No one can speak it without private language lessons, and the ability to use it has become a requirement for everything from college to the workplace.

2000-02-01.title: Montreal Journal; Trudeaumania Lingers but the Policies Fade Fast
2000-02-01.abstract: Mystique of former prime minister Pierre Elliott Trudeau remains strong among Canadians, three decades after Trudeaumania swept country; has been fed partly by celebrations for his 80th birthday, his reclusiveness and sympathy for death of youngest son; he ranks as best prime minister of century in poll of Canadians, who also rank him fourth in poll for person of millennium; while his big-government economic policies are in retreat and his bilingual policy has failed to blunt Quebec separatism, preoccupation with his legacy reflects how much Trudeaumania stamped two generations that will lead Canada into new century; photo (M)i

2000-02-01.title: Russians Wary of U.S. Pitch For Missile Defense System
2000-02-01.abstract: Sec of State Madeleine K Albright fails to convince Russia's Foreign Min Igor S Ivanov during Moscow visit to accept new American missile defense system proposed by Pres Clinton year ago; Russians remain wary of American assertions that threat from nations such as North Korea, Iran and Iraq is real and justifies amending cold war's basic arms control treaty to allow building limited national missile defense system (M)

2000-02-02.title: If Democracy Is Not Enough
2000-02-02.abstract: Roger Cohen analysis holds that European Union demonstrated its abrupt development into serious political force when it demanded that Austria keep party of Jorg Haider, rightist politician, out of government (M)

2000-02-02.title: Indonesian General Denies Guilt in Timor Abuses
2000-02-02.abstract: Indonesian Gen Wiranto denies that he was responsible for human rights abuses in East Timor and sidesteps suggestion by Pres Abdurrahman Wahid that he resign (M)

2000-02-02.title: HOUSE APPROVES EXPANSION OF TIES TO TAIWAN'S ARMY
2000-02-02.abstract: House votes, 341 to 70, to expand military ties between Taiwan and US, move that China and Clinton administration warn could aggravate regional tensions and jeopardize landmark trade agreement; critics say bill will upset Washington's 'one China' policy, which treats Taiwan as part of China and recognizes Beijing as China's capital, but also allows US to provide Taiwan with defensive weaponry; senior Republicans, led by Rep Tom Delay, choose security measure as first major bill for floor consideration this year precisely because it will challenge China (M)

2000-02-02.title: U.S. Will Reconsider Ties If Haider Joins Coalition
2000-02-02.abstract: White House officials say that US will consider joining European Union in diplomatically isolating Austria now that Freedom Party, which espouses anti-immigrant platform, and People's Party have agreed to form coalition government (M)

2000-02-02.title: Chechen Rebels Announce Retreat From Battered Capital
2000-02-02.abstract: Chechen rebels say they are abandoning Grozny and will carry out attacks on Russian forces from mountains and countryside of breakaway republic; map; photo (M)

2000-02-03.title: Albright Visits Post-Tudjman Croatia and Hails the New Leadership
2000-02-03.abstract: Sec of State Madeleine K Albright visits Zagreb, Croatia, and praises government of Prime Min Ivica Racan; says US will support integration of 'new Croatia' into NATO, European Union and West; photo (M)

2000-02-03.title: House Vote on Aid to Taiwan Angers China
2000-02-03.abstract: China protests vote in US House of Representatives to expand military ties with Taiwan, calling it 'serious encroachment on China's sovereignty' (M)

2000-02-03.title: In 3 Capitals, Leaders Seek Ulster Solution
2000-02-03.abstract: British and Irish leaders and Ulster politicians are working feverishly in desperate effort to save new Northern Ireland government from being shut down by dispute over paramilitary disarmament (M)

2000-02-03.title: WORLD BRIEFING
2000-02-03.abstract: ASIA    INDIA: 3 DIE IN JAYALALITHA PROTEST  At least three students were burned to death in Madras when supporters of the power broker Jayalalitha Jayaram, who earlier in the day was convicted on public corruption charges, began burning buses in protest. Jayalalitha, as she is known, is a former actress who became the chief minister of the state of Tamil Nadu. Sentenced to a year in prison, she remains free on bond pending an appeal.   Barry Bearak (NYT)    INDIA: CONFESSION REPORTED  Dara Singh, the accused murderer of an Australian missionary, Graham Staines, and his two sons, has confessed to that crime as well as the killing of a Muslim businessman, according to police officials. Mr. Singh was arrested in a forest hideout after a manhunt lasting more than a year.   Barry Bearak (NYT)

2000-02-03.title: Adrian Lajous, 79, Opponent Of Mexico's Nationalized Banks
2000-02-03.abstract: Adrian Lajous Martinez, finance official in Mexico who resigned his cabinet post in 1982 to protest nationalization of the banks, died on Jan 24 at age 79 (M)

2000-02-04.title: Britain to Retake Reins of Ulster If I.R.A. Doesn't Move to Disarm
2000-02-04.abstract: Britain moves to suspend power-sharing government in Northern Ireland by next week, but says it will intensify efforts to get paramilitary groups to begin disarming before using that authority; Northern Ireland Sec Peter Mandelson, speaking to somber session of House of Commons, says he will introduce emergency legislation to establish power to shut down two-month-old government and restore direct rule from London (M)

2000-02-04.title: U.S. Seizes Russia Tanker Said to Carry Oil From Iraq
2000-02-04.abstract: Two American warships enforcing United Nations sanctions against Iraq seize Russian tanker in Persian Gulf after boarding it and discovering evidence that it might be smuggling oil out of southern Iraq (S)

2000-02-04.title: RIGHTISTS TO JOIN AUSTRIA'S CABINET
2000-02-04.abstract: Austrian Pres Thomas Klestil announces that he will swear in new government on Feb 4 that includes Jorg Haider's anti-immigrant Freedom Party, setting country on collision course with European Union, which regards Haider's party as xenophobic and extremist; Klestil's decision bows to logic of Austrian democracy in that Freedom Party and mainstream conservative People's Party led by Wolfgang Schussel won enough seats in October to govern; but it also gives stature of government office to a rightist party that has not hesitated to employ bigotry and vitriol; photo of Klestil, Haider and Schussel (M)

2000-02-04.title: Muzzle Chafes Chechen War Media
2000-02-04.abstract: Two struggles are under way in Chechnya: fierce fight between Russian military and thousands of rebels, and more subtle tug of war between Russian high command and nation's media; every day Russia's journalists do battle with military press service, which regulates access to battlefield and is determined to put out message that Kremlin's war is going according to plan (M)

2000-02-04.title: World Briefing
2000-02-04.abstract: EUROPE    ITALY: ELECTION REFORM VOTE -- The constitutional court gave the go-ahead to seven referendums between April and June, including one that its supporters say would end political instability by abolishing election by proportional representation to a quarter of the seats in Parliament's 630-member lower house. In a vote last April, some 90 percent favored doing away with proportional representation. But the ballot was declared void when the turnout reached only 49.6 percent.   Elisabetta Povoledo

2000-02-05.title: Arms Inspector Seeks Bigger U.N. Role in Iraq
2000-02-05.abstract: United Nations' new chief arms insepctor for Iraq, Hans Blix, says his inspection teams will not force their way into suspected weapons sites in Iraq but would defer to UN headquarters to resolve any confrontations; Blix's intention to bring inspection system into closer alliance with UN bureaucracy differs from his predecessors, who kept their operations largely independent (M)

2000-02-05.title: Tight Space. No Privacy. Soviet Decor.
2000-02-05.abstract: Ten million Russians, including one million in St Petersburg, still live in Soviet-era communal flats, makeshift cubicles crammed with people and utterly devoid of privacy; residents share bathrooms, hallways and common kitchen; they are mired in communal milieu by inertia, lack of choice and outright poverty; photos; diagram (M)

2000-02-05.title: Indonesia Army Chief Says He Opposes a Coup
2000-02-05.abstract: Jakarta, Indonesia, has been swept by rumors of coup since special commission recommended indictment of most prominent general, Wiranto, as one of officers responsible for carnage in East Timor last year; Wiranto has proclaimed his innocence and ignored Pres Abdurrahman Wahid's requst to resign his cabinet post; Defense Min Juwono Sudarsono says he has been instructed by president to ask Wiranto directly to step down; issue will have to wait until Wahid returns from trip abroad; Gen Tyasno Sudarto, army commander, says standoff has created dangerous situation but that he is lobbying with his men not to stage coup; photo (M)

2000-02-05.title: Ex-Premier Primakov Drops Out Of Presidential Race in Russia
2000-02-05.abstract: Former Prime Min Yevgeny M Primakov takes himself out of running in Mar 26 presidential election; says Russia still has long way to go before becoming civil society and true democracy; Primakov was shoved onto sidelines when Vladimir V Putin became acting president after Boris Yeltsin's resignation on Dec 31; Putin has soared in polls and he now is expected to win presidential election (M)

2000-02-05.title: South African Leader Warns Unions Not to Impede Development
2000-02-05.abstract: Pres Thabo Mbeki, addressing South African Parliament in his first state of the nation speech, vows to relax restrictive labor laws, speed pace of privatization and reduce spending in bloated public sector; Mbeki highlights country's brightening economic fortunes and warns that he will not allow organized labor to threaten continued growth; labor has long supported Mbeki's African National Congress but opposes its fiscally conservative policies; Mbeki's remarks are clearly made with eye to investors abroad, who have complained about South Africa's tough labor laws, which make it difficult to transfer, reassign or fire employees; unemployment stands at nearly 40 percent (M)

2000-02-06.title: Economic Problems Hinder Croatia's Effort at Changes
2000-02-06.abstract: Prime Min Ivica Racan's new Croatian government moves to keep its promises of social equality and justice, with plans to cut official salaries by 40 percent and to arrest former Cabinet Min Ivan Herak and tycoon Miroslav Kutle for embezzlement; Racan says economic situation is worse than he imagined after nine years of rule by party of late Pres Franjo Tudjman (M)

2000-02-06.title: U.S. ANTIDRUG PLAN TO AID COLOMBIA FACES SKEPTICISM
2000-02-06.abstract: Clinton administration's $1.3 billion plan to help Colombia fight drug trafficking and leftist insurgents faces skepticism from military and law-enforcement officials concerned that United States could be dragged into long and costly struggle that may ultimately have little impact on drug trade; aid plan is intended to reduce booming production of cocaine and heroin in Colombia, strengthen government and help it take control of large part of its southern territory now dominated by rebels; senior American defense officials say privately that they worry that American military, while engaged in antidrug effort, may be dragged deeper into Colombia's 40-year-long civil war; photo (M)

2000-02-06.title: In Kosovo, More Clashes Between Peacekeepers and Albanians
2000-02-06.abstract: United Nations peacekeepers clash with Kosovo Albanians for second day in Mitrovica, pushing back angry crowds from bridge that divides city; two Albanians and eight soldiers are wounded in clash (M)

2000-02-06.title: Vienna Riots Leave 50 Hurt As Protests Rise
2000-02-06.abstract: Clashes between police and demonstrators leave over 50 people injured in Vienna after coalition government including members of Jorg Haider's anti-immgration Freedom Party is sworn in; photo (M)

2000-02-06.title: EgyptAir Pilot Seeking Asylum
2000-02-06.abstract: EgyptAir officials say Hamdi Hanafi Taha, pilot claiming to have information about crash of airline's Flight 990 last Oct, has requested asylum in Britain (M)

2000-02-07.title: Rights Group Says NATO Killed 500 Civilians in Kosovo War
2000-02-07.abstract: Study by Human Rights Watch says NATO air campaign in Kosovo caused deaths of 500 civilians in 90 separate attacks, more than Pentagon has acknowledged but much lower than claimed by Yugoslavia; rejects any notion that NATO committed war crimes but argues that in waging war to halt Serbs from killing or driving out ethnic Albanians, alliance officials themselves violated Geneva Convention (M)

2000-02-07.title: A Peaceful Raid Ends Students' Long Siege in Mexico
2000-02-07.abstract: Mexican federal police seize main campus of National Autonomous University of Mexico in Mexico City, peacefully ending takeover by students that began in April 1999 in protest against plan to charge tuition for first time; arrest more than 600 students; Pres Ernesto Zedillo says action was taken after government concluded that leader of anarchic student movement would never agree to negotiated solution; photo (M)

2000-02-07.title: Haider Opens Old Wound Over Germans Czechs Ousted
2000-02-07.abstract: Jorg Haider, rightist leader whose Freedom Party is part of new Austrian government, says ethnic Germans expelled from what was Czechoslovakia at end of World War II should be entitled to same compensation as Austrian Jews persecuted by Nazis; statement reopens old grievance among Germans; centers on expulsion from their homes or imprisonment in Soviet Union of millions of Germans in aftermath of Hitler's war and lack of compensation for their losses or suffering; remarks seem certain to intensify international unease over Haider, whose intermittently revisionist views have included statements--later apologized for--praising aspects of Hitler's rule (S)

2000-02-07.title: First Woman Is Elected Governor in Japan
2000-02-07.abstract: Fusae Ota, former civil servant from Prime Minister Keizo Obuchi's party, becomes first woman to win governorship in Japan in race that is regarded as important gauge of support for Obuchi; she won victory in Osaka, Japan's second largest prefecture (S)

2000-02-07.title: Big Kickbacks Under Kohl Reported
2000-02-07.abstract: German prosecutors estimate more than $70 million was illegally paid in kickbacks to middlemen on sales of military and oil refinery equipment during tenure of former Chancellor Helmut Kohl, whose once glowing reputation has already been battered by revelations of secret party financing; findings--part of investigations in Germany, Switzerland and France--suggest possible corruption at highest levels of Kohl government potentially more damaging to Kohl than unrelated illegal party contributions he has already acknowledged; new allegations discussed; photo (M)

2000-02-08.title: France Provides Morning-After Pill to Schoolgirls
2000-02-08.abstract: France gives school nurses right to dispense emergency contraception pills to students, becoming first country in world to do so; pills will be available at high schools and junior high schools, where students are as young as 12 years; although nurses are advised to make efforts to inform child's parents, they are not required to; French health and education officials hope policy will help reduce unwanted pregnancies and lower country's abortion rate, among highest in European Union; while measure faces opposition from some parents and family rights groups nationwide, its has found wide supporters in working-class city of Trappes on outskirts of Paris, where many of students come from North African immigrant families who rarely discuss sex with children; women's groups, abortion rights activists and some parents groups see it as major step toward making contraception more accessible to young people; map (M)

2000-02-08.title: U.N. Increases Peacekeepers in Sierra Leone
2000-02-08.abstract: UN Security Council increases peacekeeping force for Sierra Leone to 11,000 from about 6,000 and tells troops not to shrink from using weapons against brutal rebel army; expanded peacekeeping mission, approved unanimously, becomes organization's largest (S)

2000-02-08.title: Croatia Elects a Moderate To Follow the Tudjman Era
2000-02-08.abstract: Stipe Mesic is elected to succeed late Franjo Tudjman as president of Croatia; is former leader of collective presidency before Yugoslavia disintegrated in 1991; won 56.2 percent of vote, compared with 43.8 percent for rival, Drazen Budisa; photo (M)

2000-02-08.title: WORLD BRIEFING
2000-02-08.abstract: THE AMERICAS    CANADA: QUEBEC VOTE RULED OUT -- Premier Lucien Bouchard of Quebec gave a cold shoulder to separatist hard-liners at a Parti Quebecois convention, saying his party's main goal should be to win re-election before its mandate expires in 2003 rather than to hold a third referendum on independence. Previously, he promised to hold a vote on separation. But opinion polls indicate that support for independence and his separatist party have sagged to the 35-40 percent range.   James Brooke (NYT)    MEXICO: 745 ARRESTED -- A total of 745 people, including 83 minors, were arrested when the federal police took over the national university to end a nine-month student strike, justice officials said. Several dozen were released on bail but three strike leaders, Alejandro Echavarria, Alberto Pacheco and Mario Benitez, were charged with felony crimes and denied bail. More than 7,000 people joined a spontaneous march in Mexico City to demand the prisoners' release, a call echoed by many intellectuals and opposition leaders.   Julia Preston (NYT)

2000-02-08.title: Islamists' Policies Win Allies in Malaysia State
2000-02-08.abstract: KUALA TERENGGANU, Malaysia—  Side-by-side in the corridors of the state capital building here are men wearing flowing robes and pin-striped suits, turbans and silk ties.

2000-02-09.title: Accord Ends Boycott By Japan Opposition
2000-02-09.abstract: Opposition politicians end 11-day boycott of Japan's Parliament; drop their demand that Prime Min Keizo Obuchi apologize for pushing through without debate bill to reduce number of Parliament seats (S)

2000-02-09.title: Pinochet Foes Are Granted An Appeal
2000-02-09.abstract: Gen Augusto Pinochet's hopes of being freed from house arrest and allowed to return home to Chile suffer setback when British court says it will hear appeal of Britain's decision that he is not medically fit to stand trial on torture charges; six human rights groups and Belgium government are challenging Home Sec Jack Straw's refusal to make public examination of Pinochet in Jan by British doctors (M)

2000-02-09.title: Friends Fear for Radio Reporter Still Missing in Chechnya
2000-02-09.abstract: Colleagues of Andrei Babitsky, Russian reporter for Radio Liberty, appeal for information about him five days after Russia announced that it had handed him over to Chechen rebels (M)

2000-02-09.title: Hijackers Still Hold Jet, But Make No Demands
2000-02-09.abstract: Over 150 passengers spend their third night aboard hijacked Afghan airliner, held prisoner at Stansted, England, airport by armed gang that perplexes police by giving no explanation for their seizure of plane or making any political demands; police say four members of flight crew escaped and one hostage was released on Feb 8 (M)

2000-02-09.title: Mexican Police Release 654 Arrested in Raid on University
2000-02-09.abstract: Mexican justice authorities, moving to blunt protests against police occupation of national university, free 654 of 745 people arrested in raid that ended nine-month student strike (M)

2000-02-10.title: Hijacking Over; Everyone Safe On Afghan Jet
2000-02-10.abstract: Tense standoff between police and hijackers of Afghan airliner at Stansted airport outside London ends peacefully with hijackers releasing 150 or so people aboard plane and surrendering to authorities; police say they have not identified hijackers and refuse to discuss their demands (M)

2000-02-10.title: Chancellor Proposes to Compensate Austria's Wartime Slaves
2000-02-10.abstract: Austrian Chancellor Wolfgang Schussel, trying to calm foreign fears about his coalition with rightist Freedom Party, presents his government's platform to Parliament and says he wants to compensate people forced into slavery in Austrian factories during World War II (M)

2000-02-10.title: Big Business In Line to Get Large Tax Cut From Schroder
2000-02-10.abstract: Chancellor Gerhard Schroder approves draft legislation that would slash taxes and open way for German corporations to sell off extensive cross-holdings that have tended to hinder rise of open competition and shareholders' culture (M)

2000-02-10.title: India Politician's Jewels Glare at Her Latest Trial
2000-02-10.abstract: Eight corruption cases have been filed against Jayalalitha Jayaram, former chief minister of Tamil Nadu, India; criminal court judge in Madras has been cataloging her jewelry collection for forthcoming trial (M)

2000-02-10.title: Serbs Nurse Rage After Attack in Kosovo City
2000-02-10.abstract: Serbs in Mitrovica, Kosovo, say Serbian mobs that recently rampaged through city, intimidating Albanians and leaving eight dead, were reacting to grenade attack on Serbian cafe that wounded 15 young people; map; photo (M)

2000-02-11.title: Lebanon Fighting Ebbs in Claims of Victory
2000-02-11.abstract: Fighting in Lebanon subsides as people in northern Israel emerge from bomb shelters and US calls meeting of monitoring group to curb hostilities; Israel and Iranian-backed Hezbollah declare victory in latest round of violence, indication that it may be coming to end; photo (M)

2000-02-11.title: China Reportedly Detains 2,000 Members of Falun Gong Sect
2000-02-11.abstract: Information Center of Human Rights and Democratic Movement in China says at least 2,000 followers of banned Falun Gong spiritual movement have been detained in China in last week, official holiday period for Lunar New Year; says Tracy Zhao, Beijing-born US citizen who did not have her passport when she was arrested, remains in custody (M)

2000-02-11.title: French Prosecutor Seeks Graft Trial for Ex-Foreign Minister
2000-02-11.abstract: French prosecutor Pierre Dintilhac asks that former Foreign Min Roland Dumas, his former mistress Christine Deviers-Joncour and six other people stand trial on corruption charges stemming from sex-and-kickback scandal involving former national oil company, Elf Aquitaine (M)

2000-02-11.title: British Troops Replace French in Riot-Torn Kosovo Town
2000-02-11.abstract: British peacekeeping forces take over guard duties on bridge in Mitrovica, Kosovo, replacing French troops that have been accused of failing to protect ethnic Albanians and Serbs; map; photo (M)

2000-02-11.title: Socialist Is Charting Middle Way For Chile
2000-02-11.abstract: Pres-elect Ricardo Lagos is showing strong steak of independence from his party leadership and has appointed relatively conservative, American-educated economic team that promises to keep down budget deficits and inflation in Chile; has appointed orthodox economists Jose de Gregorio as economy minister and Nicolas Eyzaguirre as finance minister (M)

2000-02-12.title: Resenting African Workers, Spaniards Attack
2000-02-12.abstract: Several towns in southeastern Spain are overwhelmed by mobs of residents chasing Moroccan and other African immigrants through streets; hundreds of Morocccan farm workers are beaten and scores of their shanties are set afire; many immigrants flee to nearby hills; map; photo (M)o

2000-02-12.title: New Violence Ends Lull in South Lebanon
2000-02-12.abstract: Shiite Muslim guerrillas kill another Israeli soldier in southern Lebanon, and Israeli warplanes respond with air strikes at suspected guerrilla targets; map; photo (M)

2000-02-12.title: BRITAIN SUSPENDS SELF-GOVERNMENT IN NORTH IRELAND
2000-02-12.abstract: Britain suspends power-sharing government of Catholics and Protestants in Northern Ireland because of Irish Republican Army's refusal to begin disarming; IRA, in promising late development, comes up with long-awaited plan for dismantling its arsenal; British move represents gravest threat to political stability that Ulster has experienced since signing of peace settlement in April 1998; officials try to dampen fears over consequences and play up importance of IRA offer (M)

2000-02-12.title: Asylum Issue After Hijacking Creates Headache for Britain
2000-02-12.abstract: Jet chartered to fly Afghan hostages home arrives in Britain; government says it does not know how many people from Ariana Airlines hijacking want to fly back and how many will ask to stay in Britain (M)

2000-02-12.title: Zimbabwe Constitution Vote Is a Showdown for Mugabe

2000-02-13.title: Congo's War Overshadows Tribal Fight
2000-02-13.abstract: Warfare between Hema and Lendu tribes in northeastern Congo spins out because the much larger fighting inside the country has so weakened the government that it cannot intervene effectively; there are also allegations that rogue soldiers from neighboring Uganda have not only sided with the Hema in conflict but have also killed Lendu for hire; Hema-Lendu conflict is fought mostly with machetes and barbed arrows; photos; map (M)

2000-02-13.title: Europe Looks for Reassurance as U.S. Pursues a Missile Shield
2000-02-13.abstract: Britain and other NATO allies of US express increasing concerns about Clinton administration's plans to negotiate with Russia to allow deployment of a missile shield, and about how much the Europeans are being consulted in the matter; a key question about national missile defense is whether the threat from nations like North Korea justifies deployment and amending the Anti-Ballistic Missile Treaty of 1972, an accord widely seen as a cornerstone of arms control (M)

2000-02-13.title: A Politician Sees Future Of Japan In Its Past
2000-02-13.abstract: Shizuka Kamei, policy chief of Japan's governing Liberal Democratic Party, emerges as prime force opposing globalization, deregulation and liberalization of country's economy and as champion of traditional ways of doing business; he most recently prevailed upon government to scuttle quietly an important component of its longstanding plans to reform banking sector; critics say Kamei's efforts on behalf of the little man and depressed industries are motivated less by principle than by political and financial calculations (M)

2000-02-13.title: Nuclear Rivals Marshal Armies of Lobbyists in Washington
2000-02-13.abstract: Pakistan and India each assemble a team of lobbyists in Washington; India, which used to eschew lobbying, has hired Verner, Liipfert, Bernhard McPherson & Hand as well as former Congressman Stephen J Solarz; Pakistan has hired former Congressman Charlie Wilson and firm of Patton, Boggs & Blow; Pakistan's team has already won one skirmish, and the two sides are now engaged in another over whether Pres Clinton should visit Pakistan as well as India in March (M)

2000-02-13.title: U.S. Victims of Chile's Coup: The Uncensored File
2000-02-13.abstract: Censored portions are restored to previously released State Department files on Charles Horman and Frank Teruggi, two Americans who were killed in Chile in 1973, and new material reveals, as the censored version did not, that department concluded almost from the beginning that government of Gen Augusto Pinochet killed the men and that it would not have done so without a green light from American intelligence; CIA releases a 22-year-old letter denying any role by agency and says it will show public files on case in spring; Freedom of Information Act, under which files were first released, allows censoring on grounds of national security and executive privilege, but restored material suggests that it was withheld so as not to embarrass government; photos (L)

2000-02-14.title: NATO-LED  TROOPS CAUGHT IN BATTLE OVER KOSOVO TOWN
2000-02-14.abstract: French troops kill Albanian gunman as fierce fighting continues in streets of Mitrovica, Kosovo, in most sustained clashes involving NATO peacekeepers since arrival in June 1999; more than dozen people are wounded, including two French soldiers, and 17 are arrested; French, Italian and British troops come under fire from snipers; former commander of Kosovo Liberation Army says Serbs attempted to attack Albanian area and that fighters of outlawed guerrilla force fought back to defend it; both Serbs and Albanians regard Mitrovica as last battlefield in war for Kosovo; thousands of Albanians, still unable to return to homes, consider Mitrovica last place still to be liberated from Serbian persecution; Serb community leader blames Albanians; both sides accuse other of wanting to control area beacuse of presence of iron ore and precious-metals mines; photos; maps (M) (M)

2000-02-14.title: Japan Grapples With a Breakdown of Discipline in Class
2000-02-14.abstract: TOKYO—  Neat ranks of immaculate schoolchildren in sailor suit uniforms bowing to their teacher at the start of class is the standard image of school life in Japan. But teachers and pupils talk increasingly of a "collapsed class" syndrome which can range from small incidences of lax discipline to violence and even murder at schools.

2000-02-14.title: As the Pariah of Europe, Austria Seems Unbowed
2000-02-14.abstract: European Union is proving unimpressive in efforts to bring Austria to its diplomatic knees following inclusion of Freedom Party of Jorg Haider, alleged Nazi-sympathizer, in coalition government; support for its public threat to isolate Austria has cooled after Austria promptly called its bluff; aggressive public posturing may have exposed Union to series of humiliating and very public compromises as Haider's prediction that censure effort would quickly crumble seems to have proved right; small and large countries alike are expressing skepticism (M)

2000-02-14.title: Arrests Add to Concern About Zimbabwe Constitutional Vote
2000-02-14.abstract: Concern grows over outcome of two-day referendum on Zimbabwe's new constitution following arrest of Tendai Biti and other members of opposition Movement for Democratic Change and allied National Constitutional Assembly; referendum is marked by charges of sporadic intimidation and voting irregularities; Biti and colleagues are reportedly arrested while urging people to vote down proposed constitution, which would strengthen power of presidency and give government right to seize white-owned land without compensation; fate of proposed constitution is seen as crucial test for longtime president, Robert Mugabe, as nation suffers through worst economic crisis since winning independence from Britain in 1980 (M)

2000-02-14.title: Zero-Tolerance Injustices Multiplying, Critics Say : A Backlash In the U.S.
2000-02-14.abstract: WASHINGTON—  Some of the stories seem so extreme, so unbelievable, so contrary to basic good sense that they have the feel of urban legend.

2000-02-15.title: GUILTY PLEAS SEEN IN THE LAUNDERING OF RUSSIAN MONEY
2000-02-15.abstract: Former Bank of New York official Lucy Edwards and husband Peter Berlin, major suspects in federal investigation into illegal transfer of billions of dollars from Russia through bank, will reportedly plead guilty to money laundering charges; will enter plea on several criminal counts, including conspiracy to commit money laundering, visa fraud, wire fraud and bribery of bank official; will forfeit $8 million seized in personal accounts by federal authorities; charges are most serious to arise from investigation that began in fall of 1998 when officials in Britain and US discovered that billions of dollars were passing through handful of accounts at Bank of New York; reviewed; photo (M)

2000-02-15.title: Factions Scuffle as Mexican Campus Reopens
2000-02-15.abstract: Scuffles break out at national university in Mexico City as tens of thousands of students return for first official day of classes since strike started in April 1999 (S)

2000-02-15.title: Turkey Accused of Arming Terrorist Group
2000-02-15.abstract: Growing scandal in Turkey stemming from crackdown on religious terror group Hizbullah leads to accusations that group may have received weapons from Turkish government; several leading politicians and news commentators charge organization has worked with military in its war against Kurdish rebels; evidence suggests Hizbullah used weapons in recent shootout in eastern provincial capital of Van, in which five police officers and two suspected militants were killed; photo (M)

2000-02-15.title: Russians Order Grozny Residents To Leave, Sealing Off Ruined City
2000-02-15.abstract: Russian commanders order Grozny, devastated capital of breakaway province of Chechnya, emptied of surviving residents and sealed; say city has become too dangerous for inhabitants, but concede move is also precaution against rebel infiltration; city will be off limits for two weeks while troops comb it for weapons, unexploded bombs and booby traps; many residents say they have no intention of abandoning city; say they have no place to go, and little desire to end up in overcrowded refugee camps; photo; maps (M)

2000-02-15.title: 13 Are Charged in Hijacking of Afghan Jet as 73 Seek Asylum
2000-02-15.abstract: British authorities charge 13 Afghans with hijacking Ariana Afghan Airlines jetliner with more that 150 people aboard during domestic flight and forcing it on odyssey across Middle East and Europe before ending saga at Stansted Airport outside London; 13 men, ranging in ages from 18 to 36, could face long prison sentences; British authorities question 14th suspect; say six others arrested in connection with hijacking were not charged and will join group of 73 passengers applying for political asylum (M)

2000-02-16.title: British Court Orders Disclosure of Pinochet's Medical Records
2000-02-16.abstract: Britain's High Court orders medical report on Gen Augusto Pinochet's health given to four countries seeking his extradition to face criminal charges; decision extends Pinochet's enforced stay in England; report had prompted Home Sec Jack Straw to say that he was inclined to end ailing 84-year-old former dictator's 16-month house arrest in England and let him return to Chile (M)

2000-02-16.title: Vatican and P.L.O. Sign Pact Guaranteeing the Church's Rights
2000-02-16.abstract: Vatican, seeking to secure rights of Roman Catholic Church in future Palestinian state, signs agreement with Palestine Liberation Organization that will protect freedom of religion and legal status of Christian churches; document is signed by Palestinian and church representatives before Pope John Paul II meets with Palestinian leader Yasir Arafat in Vatican (M)

2000-02-16.title: New Argentine President Orders Purge of 'Dirty War' Remnants
2000-02-16.abstract: Argentine Pres Fernando de la Rua orders purge of military and civilian intelligence apparatus that will eliminate many of last suspected participants of notorious 'dirty war' of 1970's and 80's from government payroll; over 1,500 military and civilian intelligence agents have been dismissed or forced to retire recently (M)

2000-02-16.title: Senior Bank Official Took Bribe, U.S. Says
2000-02-16.abstract: Justice Dept has evidence that senior Bank of New York official accepted bribe from Peter Berlin and Lucy Edwards, husband and wife implicated in money laundering investigation at bank; law enforcement officials say identity of banker cannot be determined; say it was someone senior to Edwards, former Bank of New York vice president who, along with Berlin, are expected to plead guilty to money laundering charges; Berlin, through his company, Benex International, controlled several accounts at bank that moved billions of dollars from Russia between 1996 and last year (M)

2000-02-16.title: WORLD BRIEFING
2000-02-16.abstract: ASIA    BANGLADESH: STRIKE LEADER  The opposition leader Begum Khaleda Zia began the latest of the dozens of strikes she has led in recent years to try to bring down the government of Prime Minister Sheik Hasina Wajed. The latest started as a new law takes effect that would impose jail terms of up to 14 years for blocking transportation and destroying property -- acts common during the strikes.   Celia W. Dugger (NYT)

2000-02-17.title: Crisis Session on Ulster Ends, Still Divided on Arms Issue
2000-02-17.abstract: Sinn Fein pres Gerry Adams demands that Britain restore suspended Northern Ireland government are met with counterdemands from other participants in crisis session at 10 Downing Street that Irish Republican Army first return to disarmament talks; meeting of Roman Catholic and Protestant politicians with Prime Min Tony Blair and Ireland Prime Min Bertie Ahern adjourns still divided over issue; photo (M)

2000-02-17.title: Hezbollah Not Moved By Barak Vow To Pull Out
2000-02-17.abstract: Hezbollah leader Sheik Hassan Nasrallah says his forces in southern Lebanon will continue to kill Israeli soldiers despite Prime Minister Ehud Barak's pledge to make unconditional withdrawal from area by July 7 (M)

2000-02-17.title: Banker and Husband Tell Of Role in Laundering Case
2000-02-17.abstract: Former Bank of New York vice pres Lucy Edwards and her husband, Peter Berlin, plead guilty in Federal District Court, Manhattan, to helping group of small but politically connected Russian banks create elaborate money laundering scheme that moved over $7 billion out of Russia through American bank; say they were paid nearly $2 million for their services; say scheme allowed Russian bankers to conduct illegal banking activities in US that circumvented scrutiny of federal banking regulators and law enforcement officials (M)

2000-02-17.title: Israel Expands Daylight Time, and a Religious Dispute Erupts
2000-02-17.abstract: Interior Min Natan Sharansky says 'summer time' will be 34 days longer in Israel this year, rescinding tradition of accommodating prayer schedules of some religious Jews (M)

2000-02-17.title: Wary Farmers Are Quiet After Victory in Zimbabwe Referendum
2000-02-17.abstract: Commercial Farmers Union, which represents biggest planters in Zimbabwe, is relieved that voters rejected draft constitution that would have given government right to seize white-owned land without compensation, but it realizes that issue of land redistribution is not going away (M)

2000-02-18.title: Honoring a Heretic Whom Vatican 'Regrets' Burning
2000-02-18.abstract: Hundreds of people gather at statue of Giordano Bruno at Campo dei Fiori in central Rome, where he was burned alive in 1600 as heretic, to commemorate man many Italians revere as martyr of free thought; Pope has marked this Holy Year as time for church to apologize for past errors and excesses, from Inquisition to persecution of Jews; Cardinal Angelo Sodano, Vatican secretary of state, says that church 'regrets' that it had resorted to violence in Bruno's case, but points out that Bruno's writings were 'incompatible' with Christian thinking, and that he therefore remains a heretic; photos (M)

2000-02-18.title: Chechens Tell of Torture in Russian Camp
2000-02-18.abstract: Three men seized by Russian troops and imprisoned at detention camp in Chernokozovo in northern Chechnya this winter tell human rights observers of beatings, torture and rapes they say took place there before they bribed their way to freedom this month; the three, who are now in neighboring Russian province of Ingushetia, give their accounts to researchers for Human Rights Watch; map; photo (M)

2000-02-18.title: Shock of Kosovo Violence May Prove Incentive for Peace
2000-02-18.abstract: Leaders in Kosovo say that explosion of violence in and around northern town of Mitrovica during past two weeks, which left 11 dead and dozens wounded, may not prove so much as disaster for Kosovo as an event that shocks and galvanizes society; immediate result has been to bring hundreds more peacekeeping troops to streets of Mitrovica to enforce curfew with foot patrols and post armored vehicles at almost every junction; photo (M)

2000-02-18.title: Fears Encircle War Games on India-Pakistan Border
2000-02-18.abstract: More than 100 Indian tanks are churning up billowing clouds of sand as they rumble across Rajasthani desert this week in war games about 60 miles from India's border with its longtime foe Pakistan; just a month before Pres Clinton visits region, Indian military's very public rehearsal of its limited-war strategy is yet another sign of heightened tensions between these nuclear-armed neighbors (M)

2000-02-18.title: Iran Election Today a Referendum on Reform
2000-02-18.abstract: Iran's parliamentary elections on Feb 18 emerge as referendum on just whose ideals were at heart of Iranian revolution 21 years ago; vote has become, as well, a popularity poll for Pres Mohammad Khatami, reformist theologian who was swept into office in 1997 but has yet to realize much of his liberal social agenda or achieve his stated goal of improved relations with United States; for Khatami supporters, election for Parliament is way to release religious monopoly's brakes on everything from free-market economy to free press; photos (M)

2000-02-19.title: Ambon Journal; A Year of Holy War Among Neighbors
W.T.O. Head Hopeful on China Entry
Kurdish Issue Threatens Turkey's Bid to Join European Union
2000-02-19.abstract: Two months of progress toward building new relationship between Turkey and European Union are being threatened by dispute over whether Turkey will allow European politicians to visit imprisoned Kurdish leader Leyla Zana; dispute suggests that Turkey's approach to its citizens of Kurdish descent may prove key obstacle in its bid to join union (M)

2000-02-19.title: Evidence of Mystery Particles Stirring Excitement and Doubt
2000-02-19.abstract: Physicists at University of Rome say they may have detected type of particle that is long-sought 'dark matter' that astronomers believe makes up some 80 percent of mass in universe; findings, if confirmed, could lead to conceptual breakthrough in physics; though astronomers have been measuring gravitational pull of dark matter since 1930's, they have never succeeded in detecting it directly; Dr Pierluigi Belli will present findings in Marina del Rey, Calif, on Feb 25 at Fourth International Symposium on Sources and Detection of Dark Matter in Universe; diagram (M)

2000-02-19.title: Some Charities Suspected of Terrorist Role
2000-02-19.abstract: US officials investigating decade of international terrorist attacks have found common thread, Islamic charities and relief organizations that they suspect are being used to move men, money and weapons across borders; say Osama bin Laden, Saudi exile charged with masterminding 1998 bombings of US Embassies in East Africa, relied on at least nine of groups in his recent operations (M)

2000-02-20.title: Corruption Charges Swirling Around Japanese Premier
2000-02-20.abstract: Corruption charges swirl around Japanese Prime Min Keizo Obuchi, following report in popular magazine that political secretary to Obuchi, Toshitaka Furukawa, had improperly acquired stock in telecommunications company that later skyrocketed in value; chief opposition party, smelling blood, demands that Furukawa appear before Parliament to explain how he acquired shares in little-known page company just before it was merged in 1988, eventually creating NTT Mobile Communications, country's leading cellular telephone company; photo of Obuchi (M)

2000-02-20.title: Korean Fights For Coalition And His Aims As a Reformer
2000-02-20.abstract: South Korean Pres Kim Dae Jung, two years into his five-year term, is locked in surprisingly ugly fight for his political life--or at least to avoid having remainder of his term turned into an ornamental presidency; depth of his predicament is driven home when one of South Korea's leading politicians compares Kim, who took office as a reformer with long history of opposition dissent, to Mao Zedong; many of Kim's critics grumble that in his two years in power he has adopted much of the imperial style of his authoritarian predecessors; on April 13, he faces elections for national assembly (M)

2000-02-20.title: Mubarak Visits Lebanon, Offering Support After Israeli Raids
2000-02-20.abstract: Egyptian Pres Hosni Mubarak makes unexpected visit to Lebanon to demonstrate Egypt's support in wake of Israeli bombing raids that destroyed three Lebanese power plants; brief visit is first by an Egyptian president to Lebanon since overthrow of Egyptian monarchy in 1952, and comes at time of angry protests against Israel and United States in Lebanon and elsewhere in Arab world (M)

2000-02-20.title: Candidates For Reform Show Gains In Iran Tallies
2000-02-20.abstract: Strategists for Iran's reformers say that early election results suggest that Pres Mohammad Khatami might have majority behind him in new Parliament to make good on his promises of creating a more liberal society after 21 years of rule by puritanical Islamic clerics (M)a

2000-02-20.title: U.N. Trade Meeting Brings Rich and Poor No Closer
2000-02-20.abstract: United Nations conference on developing nations concludes, Bangkok, Thailand, with expressions of good will but no real narrowing of differences that split meeting of World Trade Orgn in Seattle in December; delegates offer only general expressions of hope that rich and poor nations might eventually agree on formula that would allow them to share benefits of global trade (M)

2000-02-21.title: Saku Journal; A Postmodern Plague Ravages Japan's Workers
2000-02-21.abstract: Chronic fatigue and chronic fatigue syndrome have become major problems in Japan, not just in Tokyo but such tranquil places like Saku, on the edge of Japan's ski country; problems are increasingly associated with the stresses placed on Japanese society since it began its dramatic economic slide more than 10 years ago; many businessmen and workers have taken their change of fortune personally; family relationships are also under strain; photo; map (M)

2000-02-21.title: Iran Reformers' Gains Reflected In Homages to Outspoken Cleric
2000-02-21.abstract: Election returns indicate that Iran's reformers should have a significant, probably dominant, place in new Parliament, and they signal their confidence by paying homage to Abdullah Nouri, the most outspoken clerical advocate of change and democracy; Nouri is serving sentence for apostasy but is allowed home for a few days; his visitors include chief of staff of Pres Mohammad Khatami, several ministers, head of Iran's Central Bank, and a few provincial governors; photo (M)

2000-02-21.title: Serbs in Kosovo Town Stone U.S. Troops, Who Retreat
2000-02-21.abstract: Angry Serbs stone, punch and kick American troops in Mitrovica, Kosovo, forcing them to beat a hasty retreat and cut short their part in a citywide search for weapons; city is Kosovo's most volatile spot, just a few miles south of Serbia proper, on the divide between Albanian- and Serb-dominated parts of Kosovo; photo; map (M)

2000-02-21.title: Brazil Collides With I.M.F. Over a Plan to Aid the Poor
2000-02-21.abstract: Brazilian government plan to spend more than $22 billion on social programs in the next decade leads to squabble with International Monetary Fund, which argues that money should be used to reduce Brazil's indebtedness rather than to fight poverty; nationwide municipal elections are scheduled for October, with presidential and legislative races to follow in 2002, and the government coalition is eager to offer voters something more than continued austerity (M)

2000-02-21.title: A Mexican Village, in Clash, Brings the Police to Their Knees
2000-02-21.abstract: Sixty Mexican riot policemen are stripped of their clubs, shields and a good part of their clothes, bound together with rope and displayed on their knees in a public square in Tepatepec; enraged townspeople revolted against them after they conducted an early morning raid to end student strike at a local teachers' college; there is widespread resentment in Mexico toward police officers, who are regarded as venal, violent and overbearing; photo (M)

2000-02-22.title: The U.N.'s Unhappy Lot: Perilous Police Duties Multiplying
2000-02-22.abstract: UN officials and diplomats are increasingly concerned that dangerous international law-enforcement assignments may add up to more than organization can handle; concerns arise as agency scrambles to find thousands of police officers for operations in worldwide hot spots at time when few police departments can spare them; US Amb Richard C Holbrooke says agency is being asked to run operations on three continents in four difficult places but is not adequately staffed to do so; says solution to problem is elusive because of way UN system is structured (M)

2000-02-22.title: Chechens Tell of Murderous Rampage by Russians
2000-02-22.abstract: Refugees trickling out of Chechnya into makeshift camps in Caucasus foothills near Nazran charge Russian men hired by army to fight in Chechnya staged drunken rampage of looting and gunfire through Aldi neighborhood of Grozny, Chechen capital, where they massacred women and elderly men, looted and burned houses and animal pens to ground; say scores of residents perished; describe round-the-clock bombing of area in final days of January, when Russian troops began final assault on Gronzy; say convoy of soldiers in tanks and armored personnel carriers tossed grenades into cellars where families were hiding, forcing residents onto streets, and that soldiers shot everything alive; alleged atrocities could be one of worst civilian massacres of Chechen war; maps; photo (M)

2000-02-22.title: Albanians Rally to Oust Serbs From a City in Kosovo
2000-02-22.abstract: Thousands of ethnic Albanians from throughout Kosovo march on ethnically divided city of Mitrovica, where they clash with phalanx of NATO-led troops, who used tear gas and fists to keep them from crossing main bridge to Serbian district; tensions have flared in city in past two weeks; violence has left 11 people dead and dozens wounded; maps; photo (M)

2000-02-22.title: Hamburg Journal; For a Fresh View, Go to Germany's West Window
2000-02-22.abstract: Hamburg, Germany, is enjoying economic boom and is fast eclipsing Berlin as world-class city; has recovered its Central European hinterland, formerly cut off by Iron Curtain; is again major port of Prague and old trading links of Hanseatic League have been re-established with towns like Gdansk in Poland; photos; map (M)

2000-02-22.title: Russians Challenge U.S. Over Radar in Norway
2000-02-22.abstract: US and Russia are in drawn-out dispute over powerful American-made radar in Norway that can track missile launchings in western Russia; radar began operating in summer 1999 as part of joint US-Norwegian intelligence project near Russian border; Russians are concerned that radar, previously used in US for research into anti-missile defenses, can be linked to proposed new system to defend American territory against limited nuclear missile attacks (M)

2000-02-23.title: Lowly Backpack Turned Into Milan Must : Hasuike's No Frills Way
2000-02-23.abstract: MILAN—  Makio Hasuike, whose accessories are inspired by strawberry boxes and bra cups, cringes at the thought of frills.

2000-02-23.title: Fernando Benitez, 88, Mexican Writer, Dies
2000-02-23.abstract: Fernando Benitez Gutierrez, journalist who helped formulate new visions of Mexican identity in his penetrating reports on Indian cultures and through cultural publications that he founded, dies at age 88; photo (M)

2000-02-23.title: Consumer Goods Giant To Cut 25,000 Workers And Push E-Commerce : Unilever To Jettison Brands and Trim Jobs
2000-02-23.abstract: LONDON—  For decades Unilever Group, the world's largest consumer products company, has generated billions of dollars in steady profits by selling household brands, ranging from Dove soap and Lipton tea to Calvin Klein fragrances, in more than 100 countries.

2000-02-23.title: Russia's North: Politics and Nuclear Junk Are Hot
2000-02-23.abstract: Entire town of Gadzhiyevo, Russia, takes unprecedented action of chellenging in upcoming election referendum authority of Russian military, which plans to build dump for Navy's nuclear wastes just outside limits of closed military city; revolt is being led by former high-ranking submarine officers, who openly distrust Navy's ability to look out for their interests and protect environment; photos; map (M)

2000-02-23.title: U.S. Rejects China's Taiwan Views
2000-02-23.abstract: Clinton administration and Congress sharply criticize threat by China to forcibly reunify Taiwan with mainland if Beijing determines that negotiations between two are dragging on too long; senior lawmakers say threat could increase chances that Congress will approve legislation expanding ties between US military and Taiwan, despite Pres Clinton's veto threat (S)

2000-02-24.title: Is U.S. a Global Snoop? No, Europe Is Told
2000-02-24.abstract: European Parliament report finds two instances in which American companies gained advantage over Europeans through communications intercepted by cold-war eavesdropping network called Echelon; suggestions of commercial spying have surfaced regularly in recent years, infuriating many Europeans who suspect that military espionage systems developed in cold war would now be used to help businesses in English-speaking nations; Echelon is network of surveillance stations stiched together in 1970's by United Stations National Security Agency with Australia, Britain, Canada and New Zealand; Clinton administration and Prime Min Tony Blair of England quickly reject idea that they might be using any secret information to bolster their own economies (M)

2000-02-24.title: Pope Makes Virtual Visit To Iraqi Site He Must Skip
2000-02-24.abstract: Pope John Paul II is unwilling to surrender his dream of beginning his Holy Land pilgrimage in Iraq, so he makes 'virtual pilgrimage' to Ur, ancient city that is traditionally held to be birthplace of Abraham; Pope was forced to renounce visit to Iraq after prolonged negotiations with government of Pres Saddam Hussein broke down; instead, ceremony is held at Vatican as pope and others view film of biblical sites (M)

2000-02-24.title: Pressure Tactics Could Backfire, Candidate Warns : A Taipei Rebuff for Beijing
2000-02-24.abstract: TAIPEI—  Asserting that cross-strait relations have hit "a historically low point," a leading presidential candidate on Taiwan warned Wednesday that China might have overplayed its pressure tactics against the island.

2000-02-24.title: Dire Warnings Over China in a Taiwan Race
2000-02-24.abstract: Political opponents say election of Chen Shui-bian as president of Taiwan would cause dangerous tensions, perhaps war, with China; speculation about China's view of Chen intensify after China warns in report that any step toward formal independence, or even indefinite delay in negotiations toward reunification, could lead to military attack; Chen's party, from its founding in 1980's, has called for Taiwan's formal independence; party has moderated that stance recently, and Chen expresses exasperation at warnings about what his election might bring; insists he is peacemaker, not troublemaker; photo (M)

2000-02-24.title: Cramped Gaza Multiplies at Unrivaled Rate
2000-02-24.abstract: Population explosion in Palestinian-governed Gaza Strip appears to be unrivaled in world; its population of 1.1 million--half under 15--is expected to double by 2014, posing almost unimaginable strains on what is already highly congested area with few jobs, severely inadequate housing and almost no natural resources; population issue is unacknowledged challenge for 5-year-old Palestinian Authority; family planning is culturally sensitive and politically complex issue for young Palestinian government in conservative, male-dominated Islamic society; health and education officials are careful not to advocate population control, since that would violate Islamic teaching; photo; map; graph (M)

2000-02-25.title: INTERNATIONAL TRAVELER
2000-02-25.abstract: As part of its expanded travel coverage, the IHT today launches its International Traveler section, to appear each Friday. With city guides, health news, an international events calendar and advice for getting the best value from hotels and airlines, the section focuses on making life easier and more enjoyable for the frequent traveler. You may have already noticed the increased space devoted to travel news each day on Page 2, which began Monday. In March, the IHT will publish a revamped and enlarged quarterly travel section.

2000-02-25.title: WORLD BRIEFING
2000-02-25.abstract: EUROPE    RUSSIA: FUNERAL IN ST. PETERSBURG -- Under heavy security because of a rumored assassination plot, President Vladimir V. Putin, left, attended the St. Petersburg funeral of his mentor and that city's first elected mayor, Anatoly Sobchak. Mr. Putin said Mr. Sobchak, who fled Russia from 1997 to 1999 after being accused of corruption, was a democratic visionary destroyed by persecution. Mr. Sobchak died after a heart attack last weekend.   Michael Wines (NYT)    SERBIA: MORE POLICE FOR KOSOVO -- Responding to the need for more civilian police officers in Kosovo and for other peacekeeping missions, the United States said it would create a permanent reserve of 2,000 officers to be available without delay. ''The recent slowness in deploying desperately needed civilian police to Kosovo provides only the latest evidence that present international capabilities are not adequate,'' said Secretary of State Madeleine K. Albright.   Elizabeth Becker (NYT)

2000-02-25.title: U.S. Orders Suspension Of Gun Sales Into Canada
2000-02-25.abstract: Clinton adm suspends export of handguns, rifles and ammunition to Canada after discovering a large volume of sales in past nine months (M)

2000-02-25.title: U.S. Drug Czar Reassures Colombia on Aid
2000-02-25.abstract: Gen Barry McCaffrey, White House's top official on drug policy, caps three-day visit to Colombia by touring jungle base fropm which new Colombian Armed Forces unit hopes to attack Marxist guerrillas involved in cocaine trade; photo (M)

2000-02-25.title: China and Europeans Break Off Talks on W.T.O. Membership
2000-02-25.abstract: Chinese and European Union negotiators break off talks on China's entry into World Trade Organization, further complicating country's troubled bid to join body (M)

2000-02-26.title: 4,000 Serbs Vow to Defend Town Sector In Kosovo
2000-02-26.abstract: 4,000 Serbs hold peaceful rally in Mitrovica, Serbia, responding to earlier protest by 25,000 Albanians; pledge to defend Serbian sector of ethnically divided town; call on international peacekeepers to organize plan to allow them to return to homes; protest alleged suffering at hands of ethnic Albanians; photo (M)

2000-02-26.title: Women Win a Battle, but Job Bias Still Rules Japan
2000-02-26.abstract: Privileges of manhood remain deeply entrenched in Japan's workplace despite passage of landmark anti-discrimination law in 1985 and its reinforcement in 1999 with new amendments that include sanctions against sexual harassment; many companies still maintain separate track personnel management system for men and women, in which men are hired with general assumption that they will build careers with companies while women are still treated as adornments, or 'flowers in the workplace,' brightening up office with their presence; case of Tomoko Haneda, who won $55,000 court award in sex discrimination suit against Sharp Electronics where she began working in 1963, discussed; settlement is Japan's largest such judgment ever; photo (M)

2000-02-26.title: Europeans Deplore Executions in the U.S.
2000-02-26.abstract: Western European countries are appalled by existence of death penalty in US; many, especially France and Italy, find it contradictory that US claims to be leader in protecting human rights when it executed more than 75 people in 1999 (S)

2000-02-26.title: World Briefing
2000-02-26.abstract: ASIA    AFGHANISTAN: IMPROVING U.N. RELATIONS -- The top United Nations official in Afghanistan, Francesc Vendrell, said the organization now had offices aiding local government and health services and monitoring human rights in four major cities -- Kabul, Kandahar, Herat and Mazaar-I-Sharif. He also said women were again working with relief agencies, after being barred from jobs outside their homes.   Barbara Crossette (NYT)

2000-02-26.title: U.S. Report Harshly Criticizes China for Deterioration of Human Rights; Russia Also Faulted
2000-02-26.abstract: State Department issues annual report on human rights that sharply criticizes China; says China's poor human rights record deteriorated markedly throughout 1999, as government intensified efforts to suppress dissent; also faults Russia for indiscriminate use of force in breakaway province of Chechnya that resulted in many deaths; findings discussed (M)

2000-02-27.title: More Rainfall Adds to Woes In Wide Swath Of Africa
Czech Women Take Chisel to Stone Wall of Male Government
West Bank Standoff
2000-02-27.abstract: Analysis of deadlock in Israeli-Palestinian peace effort o

2000-02-27.title: Students in West Bank Throw Stones at the French Prime Minister
2000-02-27.abstract: Palestinian university students, angered that French Prime Min Lionel Jospin condemned as 'terrorist acts' attacks by Hezbollah guerrillas on Israeli soldiers in Israeli-occupied zone in southern Lebanon, stone his car as he leaves meeting with Palestinian intellectuals at West Bank university; violence embarrasses leaders of university, whose new law school was financed by France, and angers Palestinian leader Yasir Arafat, Jospin's host; French Pres Jacques Chirac reportedly scores Jospin (M)

2000-02-27.title: With Africa Watching, Senegal Casts Votes That Count
2000-02-27.abstract: Senegal's presidential election is being watched around Africa because it offers voters choice among four credible candidates; loss by Pres Abdou Diouf, who seeks fourth consecutive term, would be significant on continent becoming increasingly impatient with democracies that offer no real choices; map; photo (M)

2000-02-28.title: Battling in Colombia but Touring Together in Europe
2000-02-28.abstract: Officials of Colombian government and leftist Revolutionary Armed Forces of Colombia are touring Europe together, even though peace talks aimed at ending 40 years of civil conflict that has claimed 35,000 lives in past decade are stalled; unconventional arrangement is described by government official as effort to build confidence and trust between adversaries (M)

2000-02-28.title: Under Fire, Japan Sect Starts Over
2000-02-28.abstract: Interview with Fumihiro Joyu, leader of reviled Aum Shinrikyo religious sect, which carried out poison gas attack against Tokyo subway system in 1995 killing 12 people; insists sect no longer poses threat to Japanese society; photo (M)

2000-02-28.title: NATO General Hopes G.I.'s Will Return to Kosovo Town
2000-02-28.abstract: Senior UN and Western officials say Pentagon has refused to allow American troops to return to northern Mitrovica, section dominated by Serbs in divided town that has become flash point for ethnic tensions in Kosovo, Serbia; Gen Klaus Reinhardt, German commander of NATO-led peacekeeping forces who has worked to internationalize troops in Mitrovica, says negotiations are continuing; Americans have been criticized for aggressive tactics during recent weapons search, during which they were attacked by angry Serbs and withdrew; maps; photo (M)

2000-02-28.title: Smaller Is Better in State Elections in India
2000-02-28.abstract: Regional parties humble both of India's national parties in state elections, emphatically reaffirming that days when pan-India party could dominate nation's politics are over; biggest shock to ruling Hindu nationalist Bharatiya Janata Party is in Bihar, second-largest state, in which party of Laloo Prasad Yadav that has ruled Bihar for decades captured 123 seats in legislative assembly--one more than Janata and its allies combined; map (M)

2000-02-28.title: Phone Deal In Hong Kong Said to Get A New Player
2000-02-28.abstract: Rupert Murdoch's News Corporation agrees to make $1 billion investment in Singapore Telecommunications, enabling it to sweeten offer for Hong Kong's dominant telephone company, Cable & Wireless HKT Ltd (M)

2000-02-29.title: New Providence Journal; Bahamians Resent the Loss of Beaches to Luxury
2000-02-29.abstract: Long-simmering resentment among Bahamians over loss of beaches to outside developers crystallizes in opposition to proposed luxury community on longest remaining stretch of undeveloped coastline in New Providence, most heavily populated island in Bahamas chain; government of Prime Min Hubert Ingraham supports development, in keeping with policy of wooing newcomers who bring money and jobs, but opponents say their battle is not only over access to beach but also over access to their history; site of proposed development is dotted with ruins of 18th-century plantations, as well as buried artifacts of two Lucayan Indian villages that date back 1,000 years; photo; maps (M)

2000-02-29.title: Tijuana Governor Says Slaying Shows Drug Traffickers' Power
2000-02-29.abstract: Governor Alejandro Gonzalez Alcocer of Tijuana State, day after assassination of police chief, says drug traffickers are out of control partly because many federal authorities are in their pay; suggests traffickers ordered assassination of Alfredo de la Torre Marquez in response to Pres Ernesto Zedillo, who recently visited Tijuana, where he spoke out against traffickers (S)

2000-02-29.title: Haider Gives Up His Party Post, but Not Austrian Politics
2000-02-29.abstract: Austrian rightist Jorg Haider bows to international pressure and resigns as leader of anti-immigrant Freedom Party, which he has transformed from marginal fringe group into powerful national force; says move is not withdrawal from politics; says he felt overburdened with twin jobs of Carinthian governor and party leader; abrupt move reflects country's discomfort at its diplomatic isolation and suggests tactical maneuver consistent with Haider's long history of keeping his opponents off balance; photo (M)

2000-02-29.title: Internet Recharges Reformers In Korea
2000-02-29.abstract: Internet has become powerful political tool for South Korean opposition parties hankering for reform in ways unimaginable two or three years ago; more than 500 civic groups and nongovernmental organizations have shaken established parties under online banner of Citizens Solidarity; are demanding elimination of reportedly corrupt politicians from their slates in nationwide legislative election in April; are enforcing demands by posting lists of politicians deemed unworthy to hold office; are publishing reports on Internet of allegedly shady dealings by politicians, their poor attendance records in legislature and their votes for unpopular bills (M)

2000-02-29.title: WORLD BRIEFING
2000-02-29.abstract: EUROPE    NORTHERN IRELAND: MORE MEETINGS -- Britain's secretary for Northern Ireland, Peter Mandelson, met with Catholic and Protestant leaders in Belfast to try to unblock the stalled peace agreement. Britain suspended the power-sharing Ulster government Feb. 11 because of the Irish Republican Army's refusal to start disarming. Sinn Fein's president, Gerry Adams, said it was up to Britain to restore the administration. The Ulster Unionist leader, David Trimble, said it was up to Sinn Fein to make a move on I.R.A. weapons first.   Warren Hoge (NYT)    BRITAIN: PASSPORTS FOR PETS -- Britain has begun allowing animals with ''pet passports'' proving that they are free of rabies to enter the country without having to spend six months in quarantine. The animals must be fitted with an identity microchip, and be vaccinated and tested by a licensed veterinarian to ensure that the vaccine is working. If there is no outbreak of rabies in Britain within the next year, the plan, which applies to dogs and cats traveling to Britain from 22 western European countries, will be expanded to include pets from the United States, Canada and the Caribbean.   Sarah Lyall (NYT)
